Nvidia Surpasses $40B in 2026 Strategic Deals

11:52AM

★ NVDA is recommended in Stock Advisor and in Hidden Gems

Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) has accelerated its transformation into a powerhouse AI investor, surpassing $40 billion in equity bets so far this year. This week, the company secured deals to invest up to $3.2 billion in glass maker Corning (NYSE: GLW) and $2.1 billion in data center operator IREN.

These investments are central to CEO Jensen Huang’s strategy to control the AI infrastructure stack. By financing the supply chain, Nvidia ensures its hardware remains the industry standard. High-profile wins include a $5 billion stake in Intel (NASDAQ: INTC), now valued at over $25 billion, and a $30 billion investment in OpenAI.

While Wedbush analysts say these deals build a "competitive moat," some critics warn of "circular investments"—where Nvidia funds the customers buying its chips. Currently the world’s most valuable company with a $5.2 trillion market cap, Nvidia’s ecosystem-focused strategy continues to redefine the global tech landscape.

Lumentum Set to Replace CoStar in Nasdaq-100

11:40AM

Lumentum Holdings (NASDAQ: LITE) is slated to join the Nasdaq-100 Index before markets open on May 18, 2026. The photonics and optical technology firm will replace real estate services provider CoStar Group (NASDAQ: CSGP), Nasdaq Global Indexes announced late Friday.

The move follows a stellar year for Lumentum, which has seen its market value increase ten-fold over the last 12 months. This growth stands in stark contrast to CoStar Group, which has lost nearly 57% of its value due to residential market challenges and rising competition.

Lumentum’s inclusion in the index—which tracks the largest nonfinancial firms on the Nasdaq—marks another major milestone for the company after it joined the S&P 500 last March. With over $600B in global assets tracking the Nasdaq-100, the shift represents a significant gain in institutional visibility for the tech manufacturer.

Insiders Buy GEHC, TMUS, SPGI, CAT

10:25AM

Insider sentiment remained cautiously optimistic this past week as executives at several household names stepped in to buy their own stock. While institutional flows were high, significant individual purchases by directors and C-suite leaders at major firms suggest a "buy the dip" mentality in the large-cap sector.

GE Healthcare Technologies (NASDAQ: GEHC): Director H. Lawrence Culp Jr. purchased $5,000,076 worth of stock at $61.88 per share, increasing his total holdings to 492,174 shares.

T-Mobile US (NASDAQ: TMUS): Andre Almeida, Chief of Broadband, Enterprise, and Emerging, bought $1,000,016 in shares at a price of $196.18, bringing his total ownership to 44,850 shares.

S&P Global (NYSE: SPGI): CEO of S&P Dow Jones Indices Catherine R. Clay initiated a new position with a $1,078,475 purchase at $431.39 per share. Director Robert Edward Jr. Moritz also bought $500,001 worth of shares at $434.03.

Caterpillar (NYSE: CAT): Director David MacLennan purchased $219,210 worth of stock at $876.84 per share, bringing his total holdings to 8,206 shares.

Ecolab (NYSE: ECL): Director David MacLennan was active across his portfolio, also buying $205,530 of Ecolab at $256.91, raising his total stake to 28,439 shares.

Clorox Co (NYSE: CLX): Director Pierre R. Breber purchased $429,124 in stock at $85.82 per share, representing a 38% increase in his total holding to 18,000 shares.

Willis Towers Watson Plc (NASDAQ: WTW): CEO Carl Aaron Hess bought $510,160 worth of shares at $255.08, bringing his total holdings to 117,425 shares.

Floor & Decor Holdings (NYSE: FND): CEO Bradley Paulsen purchased $251,250 in stock at $50.25 per share, increasing his total position to 85,010 shares.

Oil Executives Address the New Energy Landscape

9:15AM

Over the past two weeks, executives at major oil and gas companies reported earnings—with a chance to address the ongoing disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. The blockade has already resulted in a loss of nearly one billion barrels of oil, shifting the market from a surplus into a significant deficit.

Industry leaders emphasized that "energy security" has moved from a talking point to a primary strategic driver, leading to several key shifts:

Baker Hughes (NASDAQ: BKR): CEO Lorenzo Simonelli noted a permanent move toward diversifying infrastructure to reduce reliance on single large-scale assets.

SLB (NYSE: SLB): CEO Olivier Le Peuch expects higher oil prices to persist, shifting portfolio allocations toward offshore opportunities in Africa and the Americas.

Exxon Mobil (NYSE: XOM): CEO Darren Woods stated the company is leading a global reassessment of energy security to ensure they do not have the same "exposure" to Middle Eastern disruptions in the future.The company is prioritizing "advantaged" production in Guyana and the Permian Basin to offset Middle East losses. The company is also aggressively increasing refinery throughput—adding 200,000 barrels per day in March—to stabilize supply.

Halliburton (NYSE: HAL): CEO Jeffrey Miller highlighted a "fundamentally tighter" market that will require a massive rebuilding of global inventories.

Diamondback Energy (NASDAQ: FANG): Executives pointed to U.S. crude oil reaching record export levels as it becomes a vital pillar of global energy security.

Beyond fossil fuels, companies confirmed continued aggressive investment in nuclear, geothermal, and grid modernization to ensure long-term resilience.

Copper Prices Rally on Tight Supply, AI Demand

7:12AM

Copper futures climbed above $6.24 per pound in early May 2026, nearing the all-time record of $6.58 as the market grapples with a tightening supply squeeze and surging demand from the artificial intelligence sector.

Supply conditions have deteriorated significantly, particularly in Chile, where production hit a nine-year low in February. Regional output dropped 9% in March, hampered by operational challenges and a critical shortage of sulfuric acid. This chemical scarcity is being driven by a Chinese export ban and shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, which have blocked over half of Middle Eastern sulfur shipments.

Simultaneously, the rapid expansion of AI infrastructure is fueling massive demand. Analysts note that a single large AI data center can require up to 50,000 tonnes of copper. With total data center demand expected to reach 475,000 tonnes annually by 2026, Citigroup (NYSE: C) forecasts prices will average $13,000 per metric ton through the end of the year.

While a major new mining project is under discussion in the Democratic Republic of Congo, it lacks a firm timeline, leaving the market in a structural deficit for the foreseeable future.

Weekly News: TTD Faces a Credibility Test

6:58AM

The Trade Desk (NASDAQ: TTD) shares tumbled more than 10% on Friday morning, hitting a multiyear low as the market digests a mixed Q1 report. While the company beat on revenue, a slight earnings miss and conservative guidance have taken a backseat to a brewing industry battle with advertising giant Publicis Groupe (OTC: PUBGY). TTD shares were down 4.9% for the week.
